title: Measuring economic development
summary: Growth is not the same as development. This lesson separates economic growth (more real output) from economic development (a broader, multidimensional rise in well-being), then works through how development is measured: single indicators such as GNI per capita, health and education statistics, and infrastructure access; the composite Human Development Index (HDI) and its three dimensions; other indices such as the inequality-adjusted HDI and the Gender Inequality Index; and why no single number can fully capture development.
learningObjectives: Distinguish clearly between economic growth and economic development.
Explain single indicators of development — GNI per capita, health, education, and energy/infrastructure access — and what each reveals and omits.
Explain the three dimensions of the Human Development Index (HDI) and evaluate its strengths and limitations.
Outline other composite measures (inequality-adjusted and gender indices) and explain why no single measure fully captures development.
Interpret and compare HDI values and other indicators across countries.
levelNote: Common to SL and HL at this level.

simpleExplanation.title: A Nation's Report Card, Not Just Its Bank Balance
simpleExplanation.summary: Judging a country only by its income is like judging a student on one test score. Development is the full report card — income plus health plus education — and even that report card leaves things out.
simpleExplanation.analogy: Think of a school report. A high Maths mark is good news, but it does not tell you whether the student is thriving. To see the whole picture you also want their English and Science results, their attendance, and their well-being. The Human Development Index does the same for a country: it blends income with life expectancy and schooling into one score. But like any report, an average grade can hide a struggling student sitting behind a strong class average.
steps: Separate growth (more real national income) from development (a wider rise in living standards, health, education and freedom).
Use single indicators — GNI per capita, life expectancy, schooling, access to electricity — noting what each shows and misses.
Combine them with a composite indicator: the HDI's three dimensions of health, education and income.
Recognise the limits — averages hide inequality — and why extra indices (IHDI, GII) are needed alongside the HDI.

flashcards.back: An increase in an economy's real output over time, usually the percentage change in real GDP or GNI. It is quantitative and a means to an end.
A multidimensional improvement in living standards, health, education, freedom and economic choice. It is qualitative as well as quantitative, and it is the end goal that growth serves.

flashcards.front: Growth vs development in one line
flashcards.back: Growth is more output; development is a broader, better life. Growth can occur without development if the gains are concentrated, temporary, or bought at high social or environmental cost.
Gross National Income per person, adjusted for purchasing power parity so a given income buys a comparable basket of goods across countries. A single indicator of average income.
An adjustment that equates the purchasing power of different currencies by comparing the price of a common basket of goods, allowing fairer cross-country comparison of living standards.

A measure of one dimension of development — e.g. infant mortality (health), adult literacy or mean years of schooling (education), or the share of the population with access to electricity (infrastructure).
Health: life expectancy at birth, infant/maternal mortality. Education: adult literacy rate, mean and expected years of schooling. Each captures one dimension of well-being that income alone misses.
Measures such as access to electricity, clean water, sanitation, internet or mobile coverage. They reveal the productive and living conditions that raw income figures can hide.
An index combining several single indicators into one value for a more holistic view. The HDI is the leading example.
A UN composite indicator scoring average achievement in three dimensions — a long and healthy life, knowledge, and a decent standard of living — on a scale from 0 to 1.

1) A long and healthy life — life expectancy at birth. 2) Knowledge — mean and expected years of schooling. 3) A decent standard of living — GNI per capita (PPP$). It is the geometric mean of the three sub-indices.
The HDI discounted for inequality within each of its three dimensions. If distribution were perfectly equal, IHDI = HDI; the larger the gap, the greater the internal inequality.
A composite measuring disparities between women and men in reproductive health, empowerment and labour-market participation. A higher GII means greater inequality.

flashcards.front: What the HDI does NOT capture
flashcards.back: Inequality of distribution, depth of poverty, gender gaps, political freedom, human security, cultural life and environmental sustainability. This is why complementary indices exist.
A common error is to treat rising GDP as automatic development. Development also requires the gains to raise health, education and living standards for the population, not just headline output.

quickCheck.prompt: A country's real GDP rises 6% in a year, but the gains go almost entirely to a small elite while health and schooling are unchanged. This is best described as:
options: Economic growth without much economic development
quickCheck.answer: Economic growth without much economic development

quickCheck.prompt: Two countries have identical GDP per capita. It is still possible for one to be far more developed than the other because GDP per capita:
options: Is an average that ignores health, education and distribution
quickCheck.answer: Is an average that ignores health, education and distribution

quickCheck.prompt: Which three dimensions does the Human Development Index combine?
In the HDI, a long and healthy life is measured by:
A country has a high HDI but a much lower inequality-adjusted HDI (IHDI). This tells us that:
options: Its average achievements are unequally distributed within the population
Its GNI per capita is falling
quickCheck.answer: Its average achievements are unequally distributed within the population

faq.q: Why adjust GNI per capita for Purchasing Power Parity (PPP)?
faq.a: Because the same amount of money buys different quantities of goods in different countries. A given income stretches much further where prices are low. The PPP adjustment converts incomes into international dollars of equal purchasing power, so cross-country comparisons of living standards are fairer. Without it, high-price countries can look richer in real terms than they actually are.

faq.q: If a country has a high HDI, is everyone in it well off?
faq.a: Not necessarily. The HDI is an average and can hide large internal inequalities — a wealthy, educated urban majority alongside a poor rural minority with little healthcare or schooling. This is exactly why the UN also publishes the inequality-adjusted HDI (IHDI): when the IHDI is well below the HDI, the country's achievements are shared unevenly.

faq.q: What are the main alternatives or complements to the HDI?
faq.a: The inequality-adjusted HDI (IHDI) discounts the HDI for internal inequality; the Gender Inequality Index (GII) measures disparities between women and men; the Multidimensional Poverty Index (MPI) counts overlapping deprivations at the household level; and measures such as the Happy Planet Index bring in environmental sustainability. Each captures something the HDI leaves out, which is why development is best judged with several indicators together.

faq.q: Why can no single measure fully capture development?
faq.a: Development is multidimensional — it spans income, health, education, equality, freedom, security and the environment. Any single number must leave dimensions out, and any average conceals distribution. Even a rich composite like the HDI omits inequality, political freedom and environmental damage. So economists read a dashboard of indicators rather than trusting one figure.

sections.content: Imagine two countries. Country A has a high average income, but a small elite holds most of the wealth, life expectancy is only average, and many children leave school early. Country B has a more modest average income spread more evenly, its citizens live long lives, and schooling is universal. Which is more 'developed'? Answering that question is what this lesson is about: moving beyond a single income figure to capture the fuller meaning of human development — and understanding why even our best composite measure is not the whole story.

It is essential not to use these terms interchangeably. Economic growth is the increase in a country's real national output over time — a quantitative measure, usually the percentage change in real Gross Domestic Product (GDP) or Gross National Income (GNI). Economic development is broader and qualitative: it is a sustained, multidimensional improvement in human well-being, covering health, education, living standards, and the freedom to make choices. Growth is often a necessary condition for development because it provides the resources to fund schools, clinics and infrastructure — but it is not sufficient on its own.

items: **Growth** — an increase in real GDP/GNI. It is a *means*.
**Development** — a broad rise in living standards, health, education and freedom. It is the *end*.
Growth can occur **without** development if the gains are captured by a few, are short-lived (e.g. from a single resource boom), or come at heavy social or environmental cost.
Development without growth is harder to sustain, because improving health and education over the long run needs resources that growth provides.

sections.content: A single indicator measures one dimension of development. Each is useful but partial, so economists read several together as a 'dashboard'. Income is captured by GNI per capita (PPP$). Health is captured by life expectancy at birth and by infant and maternal mortality rates. Education is captured by the adult literacy rate and by mean and expected years of schooling. Living conditions and productive capacity are captured by energy and infrastructure indicators such as the share of the population with access to electricity, clean water, sanitation, or the internet.

items: **Income:** GNI per capita (PPP$) — average income, adjusted for the cost of living.
**Health:** life expectancy at birth; infant and maternal mortality rates.
**Education:** adult literacy rate; mean and expected years of schooling.
**Energy / infrastructure:** access to electricity, clean water, sanitation, mobile and internet coverage.
Each single indicator lights up one dimension and leaves the rest dark — which is exactly why composite measures were created.

sections.question:
Country X has a Gross National Income (GNI) of 450 billion dollars and a population of 20 million. Country Y has a GNI of 1,200 billion dollars and a population of 150 million.

(a) Calculate GNI per capita for each country.
(b) State which country has the higher average income on this indicator.
(c) Explain one reason why this single indicator may still mislead about which country is more developed.
sections.solution:
**(a)** GNI per capita = Total GNI / Population.
- Country X: 450,000,000,000 / 20,000,000 = **22,500 dollars per person**.
- Country Y: 1,200,000,000,000 / 150,000,000 = **8,000 dollars per person**.

**(b)** On this single indicator, **Country X** has the higher average income (22,500 vs 8,000), even though Country Y's *total* GNI is far larger. Development depends on income *per person*, not total output.

**(c)** GNI per capita is an **average**, so it hides distribution. Country X's 22,500 could be concentrated in a wealthy elite while most citizens earn far less, whereas Country Y's income could be spread more evenly. The figure also ignores health, education, unpaid and informal work, and negative externalities such as pollution. So a higher GNI per capita does not automatically mean a more developed society — it is one dimension only.

sections.content: The best-known composite indicator is the United Nations' Human Development Index (HDI). It summarises average achievement in three dimensions of human development and reports a single score between 0 and 1: the closer to 1, the higher the development. The HDI is calculated as the geometric mean of three normalised sub-indices, which means a country cannot compensate for a very weak dimension simply by excelling in another — balanced progress matters.

items: **Dimension 1 — a long and healthy life:** measured by *life expectancy at birth*.
**Dimension 2 — knowledge:** measured by *mean years of schooling* (adults aged 25+) and *expected years of schooling* (children entering school).
**Dimension 3 — a decent standard of living:** measured by *GNI per capita (PPP$)*.
The score runs from 0 to 1, and countries are grouped into four tiers: **low, medium, high and very high** human development.

sections.content:
Conceptually, the HDI is the geometric mean of its three sub-indices:
HDI = cube root of (Health Index x Education Index x Income Index)

Because it blends income with health and education, the HDI corrects the biggest weakness of GNI per capita: it recognises that a rich country with poor health or schooling is not fully developed, and that a middle-income country with long lives and strong education may be more developed than its income alone suggests. That is its central strength — but it still leaves out inequality, gender gaps, freedom and the environment.

sections.question:
The table shows illustrative data for two countries.

| Indicator | Coastland | Highvale |
|---|---|---|
| HDI value | 0.915 (very high) | 0.535 (low) |
| Life expectancy at birth | 82 years | 61 years |
| Expected years of schooling | 17 years | 9 years |
| GNI per capita (PPP$) | 55,000 | 3,400 |

(a) Compare the level of development in the two countries using the HDI.
(b) Support your comparison with each of the three HDI dimensions.
(c) Explain one thing these HDI values still do NOT tell you about either country.
sections.solution:
**(a)** Coastland is far more developed than Highvale: its HDI of **0.915** places it in the **'very high'** tier, while Highvale's **0.535** sits in the **'low'** tier — a gap of 0.38 on a 0-to-1 scale, meaning Coastland's population is on average much healthier, better educated and better off.

**(b)** The gap shows up in all three dimensions:
- **Health:** life expectancy is 82 years in Coastland versus 61 in Highvale — 21 years longer.
- **Education:** a child in Coastland can expect 17 years of schooling versus 9 in Highvale — nearly double.
- **Income:** GNI per capita is 55,000 dollars (PPP) in Coastland versus 3,400 in Highvale — roughly sixteen times higher.
Because the HDI is a geometric mean, weakness in *all three* dimensions compounds to give Highvale its low overall score.

**(c)** The HDI values are **averages**, so they say nothing about how income, health or education are **distributed** within each country. Coastland's 0.915 could conceal sharp regional or gender inequality; Highvale's population could be more equal despite being poorer. The HDI also omits political freedom, human security and environmental sustainability. To judge distribution we would need the **inequality-adjusted HDI (IHDI)** or the **Gender Inequality Index (GII)** alongside it.

sections.content: Other measures — and why we need them

Because the HDI is an average of just three dimensions, the UN and others publish complementary indices that fill specific gaps. The inequality-adjusted HDI (IHDI) discounts the HDI according to how unequally each dimension is distributed: where distribution is perfectly equal, IHDI equals HDI; the wider the gap between them, the greater the internal inequality. The Gender Inequality Index (GII) captures disparities between women and men in reproductive health, empowerment and labour-market participation. The Multidimensional Poverty Index (MPI) counts overlapping deprivations at household level, and measures such as the Happy Planet Index bring in environmental sustainability.

items: **IHDI** — the HDI adjusted downwards for inequality within its three dimensions; the HDI-IHDI gap measures the 'loss' due to inequality.
**GII** — measures gender disparities; a higher value means greater inequality between women and men.
**MPI** — measures the incidence and intensity of overlapping deprivations (health, education, living standards) at the individual/household level.

**No single measure is enough:** development spans income, health, education, equality, freedom, security and the environment — any one number must leave dimensions out and any average hides distribution.

sections.content: When evaluating an indicator, always give a balanced argument. For **GNI per capita**: strength = simple, widely available, correlates with other outcomes; weakness = an average that hides inequality and ignores non-marketed output and externalities. For the **HDI**: strength = multidimensional, so it corrects income-only measures; weakness = it still omits inequality (the IHDI addresses this), gender gaps (the GII addresses this), political freedom and environmental damage. The examiner rewards you for naming *which* index fixes *which* gap — not just saying 'the HDI is limited'.

items: **Treating growth as development** — rising GDP is growth; development also needs the gains to raise health, education and living standards across the population. State this distinction explicitly.

**Claiming GDP/GNI per capita measures development** — it is one income *average*. It ignores distribution, non-marketed and informal output, and externalities, so it cannot on its own show how developed a country is.

**Muddling the three HDI dimensions** — the HDI is health (life expectancy), education (mean and expected years of schooling) and income (GNI per capita, PPP). Do not add inflation, unemployment or the environment; do not drop education.
**Reading an HDI value as a guarantee for everyone** — it is a national average, so a high HDI can still hide deep inequality. Bring in the IHDI or GII to discuss distribution.

**Saying the HDI captures all of development** — it deliberately omits inequality, gender, freedom and sustainability. The mark comes from explaining *why* one number cannot capture a multidimensional concept.

sections.content: Model answer — marked against the real IB markband

sections.question: Paper 1, part (a): Explain, using the Human Development Index, why composite indicators may measure development better than GDP per capita alone. [10 marks]
sections.solution:
**Model answer:**
Economic development is a multidimensional improvement in well-being — covering income, health and education — and is broader than economic growth, which is simply a rise in real output. GDP (or GNI) per capita measures only the *income* dimension: it is the average income of the population. Because it is a single, income-based average, it has two major weaknesses. First, it ignores non-income dimensions of development such as health and education; a country could have high income yet low life expectancy and poor schooling. Second, being an average, it hides the *distribution* of income, so a high figure can coexist with widespread poverty behind a wealthy elite.

A composite indicator such as the Human Development Index (HDI) addresses the first weakness directly by combining three dimensions into one score between 0 and 1: a long and healthy life (measured by life expectancy at birth), knowledge (measured by mean and expected years of schooling), and a decent standard of living (measured by GNI per capita, PPP$). Because the HDI is the geometric mean of these three sub-indices, a country cannot mask weak health or education simply by having high income — balanced progress across all three dimensions is required to score highly. This makes the HDI a fuller measure of development than income alone.

For example, a middle-income country with long life expectancy and universal schooling can achieve a higher HDI than a higher-income country where citizens die younger and leave school early. GDP per capita would rank the second country ahead; the HDI reveals that the first is more developed. In this way the composite indicator captures the multidimensional nature of development that a single income figure cannot.

*[Diagram optional here: this question is best answered in prose, but a simple bar chart contrasting two countries' HDI and its three sub-components could be included and explained.]*

The HDI is not perfect — as an average it still hides inequality (addressed by the inequality-adjusted HDI), gender gaps (the Gender Inequality Index) and it omits political freedom and environmental sustainability. But precisely because it goes beyond income to include health and education, it measures development better than GDP per capita alone.

---
**How this maps to our IB markband (Paper 1 part (a), out of 10):**
- **9-10:** the demands of the question are addressed, relevant economic theory is FULLY explained, relevant terms are used appropriately THROUGHOUT, and where appropriate a relevant diagram is included and FULLY explained.
- **7-8:** the demands of the question are explained, and relevant terms are mostly used appropriately.
- **5-6:** the demands of the question are partially addressed, relevant economic theory is PARTLY explained, and some relevant terms are used.
- **3-4:** relevant economic theory is DESCRIBED, and some relevant terms are used.
- **1-2:** the response is not relevant to the question.

**Why this answer reaches the top band (9-10):**
- ✅ Demands addressed: it explains *why* the HDI is better than GDP per capita, not just what each is.
- ✅ Theory FULLY explained: it derives the argument — GDP per capita omits dimensions and hides distribution; the HDI adds health and education and uses a geometric mean so weak dimensions cannot be masked.
- ✅ Terms used THROUGHOUT: economic development, composite indicator, GNI per capita (PPP$), life expectancy, mean/expected years of schooling, geometric mean, IHDI, GII.
- ✅ A diagram is optional here and, where appropriate, would be fully explained rather than merely drawn.
An answer that only *defined* the HDI and GDP per capita, or *described* the three dimensions without explaining why they make the HDI a better measure, would sit in the 3-4 ('described') or 5-6 ('partly explained') band. Explaining the link — not just listing components — is exactly what our marking engine rewards.

sections.content: This is precisely what the Practice button does: write your own answer to this exact question, submit it, and our marking engine grades it against this same official markband — telling you which band you reached and why. The habit that moves you up the bands: don't just list the three HDI dimensions — EXPLAIN why combining them beats income alone.

How we measure development shapes the policies that follow. Once you accept that development is multidimensional, the natural next questions are about the barriers to it and the strategies to overcome them — the focus of the rest of Unit 4. Keep two key concepts in view here: **economic well-being** (development is ultimately about human flourishing, not just output) and **equity** (averages such as GNI per capita and the HDI can hide who actually benefits, which is why distribution-sensitive measures matter).

sections.label: Get a Paper 1 (a) answer marked: use the HDI to explain why composite indicators can beat GDP per capita
